questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text: Iodine is a halogen. It can form potassium iodide and hydrogen iodide.

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Name the relationship between \(^{127}_{53}I\) and \(^{129}_{53}I\).
sub_questions.answer: They are isotopes.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: The electronic arrangement of an iodine atom is 2, 8, x, 18, y. What is x?

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: Draw the electron diagram for potassium iodide, showing ELECTRONS IN THE OUTERMOST SHELLS only.
sub_questions.answer: [IMG:ans_q1_b_0.jpg]

sub_questions.label: d
sub_questions.question_text: Suggest why an aqueous solution of hydrogen iodide can conduct electricity.
sub_questions.answer:
Hydrogen iodide ionises in water to form mobile ions. / Hydrogen iodide is an electrolyte. 
(Not accept: HI is an acid / HI contains free ions. / Incorrect ions are mentioned, e.g. water ionises to form \( H^{+} \) and \(OH^{-} \).)

sub_questions.label: e
sub_questions.question_text: In terms of bonding and structure, explain whether potassium iodide or hydrogen iodide would have a higher melting point.
sub_questions.answer:
Potassium iodide would have a higher melting point because
• melting of potassium iodide needs a lot of energy to break the strong ionic bonds between  \( K^{+} \)  and  \( \Gamma \)  ions in a giant ionic structure.
• melting of hydrogen iodide needs little energy to break the weak van der Waal's forces between molecules in a simple molecular structure.
• KI has a giant ionic structure while HI has a simple molecular structure.
- A lot of energy is needed to break the strong ionic bonds between  \( K^{+} \)  and  \( \Gamma^{-} \)  ions in KI, while little energy is needed to break the weak van der Waals' forces between HI molecules.

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 7: Redox Reactions, Chemical Cells and Electrolysis
questions.question_text:
The diagram below shows an experimental set-up in which a metal oxide  \( X_{2}O(s) \)  is decomposed upon strong heating. A silvery metal X and a colourless gas Z are formed.

[IMG:q2_img_0.png]

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: State what Z is and suggest a test for it.
sub_questions.answer:
• oxygen / O₂
• It relights a glowing splint.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: When 3.028 g of  \( X_{2}O(s) \)  is completely decomposed, 2.819 g of metal X can be obtained.

children.label: i
children.question_text:
Calculate the relative atomic mass of X.
(Relative atomic mass : O = 16.0)
children.answer:
Relative atomic mass of X = 2.819 ÷  \( \frac{[3.028-2.819]}{16} \times 2 \)
\( =108\quad(Accept:107.50-108) \)

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Suggest what X is.
children.answer: silver / Ag

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: Explain whether the decomposition of  \( X_{2}O(s) \)  is a redox reaction.
sub_questions.answer:
Yes.
The oxidation number of X decreases from +1 to 0. / The oxidation number of O increases from -2 to 0. (In terms of increase or decrease of oxidation number)
(Accept: Oxidation number of X changes from +1 to 0. / Oxidation number of O: -2 → 0)
Or,  \( X^{+} \)  gains electrons from  \( O^{2-} \) / \( O^{2-} \)  loses electrons to  \( X^{+} \) . (In terms of gain or loss of electrons)
(Accept: Silver ions gain electrons from oxide ions. Not accept: X ions / O ions)
Or,  \( X_{2}O \)  loses oxygen to form X. (In terms of gain or loss of oxygen)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text: Antacid is a drug for neutralising stomach acid. A sample of an antacid contains  \( NaHCO_{3}(s) \)  and other soluble inert substances. 1.52 g of the antacid sample was completely dissolved in deionised water to give a weakly alkaline solution. The solution was then titrated with 0.644 M HCl(aq) using a suitable indicator. 25.20 cm \( ^{3} \)  of the HCl(aq) was required to reach the end point.

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Write the chemical equation for the reaction between  \( NaHCO_{3}(s) \)  and HCl(aq).
sub_questions.answer:
\( \mathrm{NaHCO_{3}(s)+HCl(aq)\rightarrow NaCl(aq)+CO_{2}(g)+H_{2}O(l)} \)  / 
\( \mathrm{NaHCO_{3}(s)+H^{+}(aq)\rightarrow Na^{+}(aq)+CO_{2}(g)+H_{2}O(l)} \)
(State symbols not required)(Ignore incorrect state symbols)

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text:
Calculate the percentage by mass of  \( NaHCO_{3}(s) \)  in the antacid sample. 
(Relative atomic masses: H = 1.0, C = 12.0, O = 16.0, Na = 23.0)
sub_questions.answer:
no. of moles of HCl(aq) used = 0.644 × 0.0252 = 0.01623 
no. of moles of  \( NaHCO_{3}(s) \)  in the antacid sample = no. of moles of HCl(aq) used = 0.01623
percentage by mass of  \( NaHCO_{3}(s) \)  in the antacid sample
=  \( 84 \times 0.01623 \div 1.52 \times 100\% = 89.7\% \)  (Accept:  \( 89.5 - 89.7\% \) )

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: The pH of the solution at the end point of the titration was found to be between 3 and 4.

children.label: i
children.question_text: Suggest a suitable indicator for this titration and state the colour change at the end point.
children.answer:
• methyl orange
• From yellow to orange / yellowish orange / orange red
(Not accept: red)

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Suggest an instrument to measure the pH of the solution accurately.
children.answer: pH meter / data-logger connected with a pH sensor

sub_questions.label: d
sub_questions.question_text: State one advantage of taking antacids containing  \( \mathrm{Mg(OH)_2(s)} \)  over those containing  \( \mathrm{NaHCO_3(s)} \) .
sub_questions.answer: No gas is given out from the reaction between  \( \mathrm{Mg(OH)_2(s)} \)  and stomach acid, or while  \( CO_2 \)  gas is given out from  \( NaHCO_3(s) \)  and leads to uncomfortable feeling in stomach.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text: Consider the molecules  \( H_{2}O \) ,  \( BF_{3} \)  and  \( SF_{6} \) .

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: \( H_{2}O \)  molecules can form  \( H_{3}O^{+} \)  ions.

children.label: i
children.question_text:
In each of the following boxes, draw the electron diagram (showing ELECTRONS IN THE OUTERMOST SHELLS only) for a suitable chemical species to show the formation of a  \( H_{3}O^{+} \)  ion.

[IMG:q4_img_0.png]
children.answer:
[IMG:ans_q4_i_0.jpg]

1 mark for each diagram (Accept: H⁺; Not accept: [H⁺])

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Describe the formation of dative covalent bond using  \( H_{3}O^{+} \)  as an example.
children.answer: A lone pair of electrons on oxygen atom in  \( H_{2}O \)  is donated to  \( H^{+} \) to form a dative covalent bond.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Explain whether the boron atom in a  \( BF_{3} \)  molecule has an octet structure.
sub_questions.answer:
No, because boron atom in  \( BF_{3} \)  molecule has only 6 outermost shell electrons.
(Not accept: not 8 outermost shell electrons / less than 8 outermost shell electrons)

children.label: i
children.question_text: Draw the three-dimensional structure of a  \( SF_{6} \)  molecule.
children.answer: [IMG:ans_q4_i_1.jpg]

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Explain whether  \( SF_{6} \)  is a polar molecule.
children.answer: No, because \(SF_6\) molecules is symmetrical, / the polarities of the S-F bonds in \(SF_6\) cancel out.

sub_questions.label: d
sub_questions.question_text:
Explain the following increasing order of the boiling points of the three compounds:

\( BF_{3} < SF_{6} < H_{2}O \)
sub_questions.answer:
• Both molecules of  \( BF_{3} \)  and  \( SF_{6} \)  are held by weak van der Waals' forces.
• The molecular size of  \( SF_{6} \)  is larger than that of  \( BF_{3} \) , therefore the van der Waals' forces between  \( SF_{6} \)  molecules are stronger than those between  \( BF_{3} \)  molecules.
• Stronger hydrogen bonds exist between  \( H_{2}O \)  molecules.
(Accept:  \( SF_{6} \)  molecule has more electrons than  \( BF_{3} \)  molecule;
Not accept: Molecules of  \( BF_{3} \)  and  \( SF_{6} \)  are-held by intermolecular forces. / The molecular mass of  \( SF_{6} \)  is larger than that of  \( BF_{3}. \) )

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 7: Redox Reactions, Chemical Cells and Electrolysis
questions.question_text:
The following hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell and aluminium-air cell are primary cells. Their simplified structures are shown below:

[IMG:q5_img_0.png]

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: What is meant by the term ‘primary cell’?
sub_questions.answer: A cell that cannot be recharged / is non-rechargeable.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: For the above hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell,

children.label: i
children.question_text: write the half equation for the change that occurs at anode A.
children.answer:
\( H_{2}(g) + 2OH^{-}(aq) \rightarrow 2H_{2}O(l) + 2e^{-} \)
(Do not accept:  \( H_{2}(g) \rightarrow 2H^{+}(aq) + 2e^{-} \) )

children.label: ii
children.question_text: suggest one disadvantage of using this hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell.
children.answer:
Hydrogen is flammable / explosive. / (Concentrated) KOH is corrosive. /
 Hydrogen / oxygen / gas is difficult to store / transport. 
(Do not accept: Need adding hydrogen / oxygen continuously to work. / The electrode is made of platinum which is expensive. / The fuel cell is bulky.)

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: In the above aluminium-air cell, oxygen in air reacts with water to form hydroxide ions at cathode Y.

children.label: i
children.question_text: Write the half equation for the change that occurs at cathode Y.

children.label: ii
children.question_text:
The half equation for the change that occurs at the aluminium anode is as follows :

\(\mathrm{Al(s)+3OH^{-}(aq)\rightarrow Al(OH)_{3}(s)+3e^{-}} \)

Write the chemical equation for the overall reaction in the aluminium-air cell.
children.answer:
\( 4Al(s) + 3O_{2}(g) + 6H_{2}O(l) \rightarrow 4Al(OH)_{3}(s) \)
(Do not accept ionic equation.)

children.label: iii
children.question_text: Suggest how aluminium can be obtained from aluminium oxide.
children.answer:
Electrolysis of molten aluminium oxide
(Do not accept: Electrolysis of molten ore / salt)
(Accept: Electrolysis of melted / liquid aluminium oxide /
Add  \( Al_{2}O_{3}(s) \)  to HCl(aq) to give  \( Al^{3+}(aq) \)  and then add Mg(s) (but not K, Na, Ca.)

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 5: Fossil Fuels and Carbon Compounds
questions.question_text:
Consider the following chemical equation for the formation of  \( CH_{3}Cl \)  from methane and chlorine :

\(  CH_{4}(g)+Cl_{2}(g)\rightarrow CH_{3}Cl(g)+HCl(g) \)

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Name the type of reaction involved.
sub_questions.answer:
Substitution / Monosubstitution  \( \dagger \)
(Do not accept: chlorination of methane / halogenation of methane)

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: State the condition needed for the reaction to occur at room temperature.
sub_questions.answer:
Light / (diffused) sunlight / ultra-violet light / UV/ hv / radical initiator
(Do not accept: heat / use a catalyst)

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: The reaction involves three stages: initiation, propagation and termination. In the initiation stage, chlorine free radicals (Cl•) are formed from chlorine molecules.

children.label: i
children.question_text: With reference to the electronic structure, explain why a chlorine free radical (Cl•) is a reactive chemical species.
children.answer:
Chlorine free radical (is a species which) has one unshared electron / does not have the (stable) noble gas electronic configuration / does not obey the octet rule / does not have an octet structure.
(Do not accept: A chlorine free radical has 7 electrons in its outermost shell. / Chlorine free radical is highly reactive.)

children.label: ii
children.question_text:
Complete the chemical equations below by filling in a suitable chemical species in each of the following boxes :

One of the steps in the propagation stage :
\(  Cl\cdot+CH_{4}\rightarrow\boxed{\quad}+\boxed{\quad} \)
One of the steps in the termination stage :
\( \boxed{\quad}+\boxed{\quad}\rightarrow CH_{3}Cl \)
children.answer:
Cl• + CH₄ →  \( \cdot \) CH₃ + HCl
\( \cdot \) CH₃ + Cl• → CH₃Cl
(Accept:  \( CH_{3}\bullet \) )
(Do not accept answers expressed using electron diagrams.)

sub_questions.label: d
sub_questions.question_text: Explain why  \( CH_{3}Cl \)  is not the only organic product formed in the reaction between methane and chlorine.
sub_questions.answer:
Methane /  \( CH_{3}Cl \)  undergoes further substitution / polysubstitution to form  \( CH_{2}Cl_{2} / CHCl_{3} / CCl_{4} \) .
(Accept:
\( Cl_{2} \)  /  \( Cl^{\bullet} \)  can react with the H atom in  \( CH_{3}Cl \)  to give other organic products /  \( CH_{2}Cl_{2} \)  /  \( CHCl_{3} \)  /  \( CCl_{4} \) . /
Chloromethane reacts with chlorine to give  \( CH_{2}Cl_{2} \)  /  \( CHCl_{3}/CCl_{4} \) .
\( CH_{3}Cl + Cl_{2} \rightarrow CH_{2}Cl_{2} + HCl \)
Two methyl radicals react to give  \( CH_{3}CH_{3} \) . /  \( CH_{3}\bullet + CH_{3}\bullet \rightarrow CH_{3}CH_{3} \)

sub_questions.label: e
sub_questions.question_text: From the hazard warning labels shown below, circle a label that should be displayed on a gas cylinder containing methane.
sub_questions.answer:
[IMG:q6_img_0.png]

(Do not accept written answer.)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
An experiment was performed to determine the enthalpy change of neutralisation between  \( \mathrm{Ca(OH)_2(s)} \)  and  \( \mathrm{HCl(aq)} \) . 100.0 cm \( ^{3} \)  of 1.0 M  \( \mathrm{HCl(aq)} \)  was placed in an expanded polystyrene cup. The temperature of the contents in the cup was measured at half-minute intervals. Right at the third minute, 0.502 g of  \( \mathrm{Ca(OH)_2(s)} \)  was added to the cup with thorough stirring. The recordings of temperature are shown in the graph below:

[IMG:q7_img_0.png]

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Write a chemical equation for the reaction between  \( \mathrm{Ca(OH)_2(s)} \)  and  \( \mathrm{ HCl(aq)} \) .
sub_questions.answer:
\( \mathrm{Ca(OH)_2(s) + 2HCl(aq) \rightarrow CaCl_2(aq) + 2H_2O(l)} \)
\( \mathrm{Ca(OH)_2(s) + 2H^+(aq) \rightarrow Ca^{2+}(aq) + 2H_2O(l)} \)
(Accept ionic equation.)
(Do not accept:  \( \mathrm{H^+(aq) + OH^-(aq) \rightarrow H_2O(l)} \))

children.label: i
children.question_text:
By SKETCHING on the graph above, estimate the greatest temperature rise of the contents in the cup.

The greatest temperature rise = ___°C
children.answer:
[IMG:ans_q7_a_0.jpg]

(Show the extrapolation line, the vertical line (at the  \( 3^{rd} \)  minute), the horizontal line (at  \( 22.5\ ^{\circ}C \) ) and the two intersecting points); or
Show the extrapolation line and mark the coordinate (3, 24.6); or
Show the extrapolation line and  \( 24.6 - 22.5 \) .
The greatest temperature rise = 2.1 °C

children.label: ii
children.answer:
Energy released during the reaction \(=100.0 \times 1.00 \times 4.2 \times 2.1 = 882 J\)
Enthalpy change of the reaction \(=-882 \div 100 \div (0.502 \div 74.1) = -130\ kJ\ mol^{-1}\)
Enthalpy change of neutralistaion \(=-130 \div 2 = -65.0\ kJ\ mol^{-1}\)
(Accept: -65.0 to -65.33 \(kJ\ mol^{-1}\))

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text:
Standard enthalpy changes of neutralisation \( \Delta H_{n}^{\circ} \) for two reactions are given below :
\(\Delta H_n^{\theta}\ /\ kJ\ mol^{-1}\)
Reaction between \(Ca(OH)_2(s)\) and HCl(aq)-58.6
Reaction between CaO(s) and HCl(aq)-186.0
Calculate the standard enthalpy change of the following reaction. \( CaO(s)+H_{2}O(l)\rightarrow Ca(OH)_{2}(s) \)
sub_questions.answer:
\( \frac{1}{2}\mathrm{Ca(OH)_2(s) + HCl(aq) \rightarrow \frac{1}{2}CaCl_2(aq) + H_2O(l)} \)   \( \Delta H_{n}^{o} = -58.6\ kJ\ mol^{-1} \)
\( \mathrm{CaO(s) + 2HCl(aq) \rightarrow CaCl_2(aq) + H_2O(l)} \)   \( \Delta H_{n}^{o} = -186.0\ kJ\ mol^{-1} \)
or
\( \mathrm{Ca(OH)_2(s) + 2HCl(aq) \rightarrow Ca Cl_2(aq) + 2H_2O(l)} \)   \( \Delta H^{o} = -117.2\ kJ\ mol^{-1} \)
\( \mathrm{CaO(s) + 3HCl(aq) \rightarrow CaCl_2(aq) + H_2O(l)} \)  \( \Delta H_{n}^{o} = -186.0\ kJ\ mol^{-1} \)
\( \mathrm{CaO(s) + H_2O(l) \rightarrow Ca(OH)_2(s)} \)   \( \Delta H^{o} = y \)
y = -186.0 - (-58.6) × 2
= -68.8 kJ mol \( ^{-1} \)
(1 mark for writing out the two thermochemical equations or draw an enthalpy change cycle;
(Correct state symbols must be shown.)
1 mark for the calculation step;
1 mark for the answer with a negative sign and a correct unit)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text: Describe and explain the similarities and differences between the chemical principles involved in tin-plating and galvanising in the rusting prevention of iron-made objects.
questions.answer:
Chemical knowledge
Similarities (maximum 2 marks):
• Both tin-plating and galvanising involve coating / covering iron with a thin layer of metal (zinc / tin).
(Do not accept ‘plating’)
• The layer prevents iron from contacting water (and) oxygen.
## Differences (maximum 3 marks):
- Tin-plated iron will rust / rust faster when tin coating is scratched off but galvanised iron will not rust / rust slower when zinc coating is scratched off.
- When the tin coating is scratched off, tin-plated iron will rust because tin will lose electrons less readily than iron / tin is less reactive than iron / tin is lower than iron in electrochemical series (ECS) / tin is a weaker reducing agent than iron.
- When the zinc coating is scratched off, galvanised iron will not rust because zinc will lose electrons more readily than iron / zinc is more reactive than iron / zinc is higher than iron in ECS / zinc is a stronger reducing agent than iron / zinc gives sacrificial protection to iron.
## Communication mark
(Chemical knowledge = 0 to 3, communication mark = 0.
Chemical knowledge = 4 to 5, communication mark = 0 or 1.
Incomplete answer or difficult to understand, communication mark = 0.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
At a certain temperature, the equilibrium constant  \( K_{c} \)  for the following reaction is  \( 2.25 \times 10^{-2} \)  mol dm \( ^{-3} \) .
PCl \( _{5} \) (g) ⇌ PCl \( _{3} \) (g) + Cl \( _{2} \) (g)  \( \Delta H > 0 \)
In an experiment, 0.84 mol of  \( PCl_{5}(g) \) , 0.16 mol of  \( PCl_{3}(g) \)  and 0.16 mol of  \( Cl_{2}(g) \)  were initially introduced in a closed container of a fixed volume of 4.0 dm \( ^{3} \) , and the system was allowed to attain equilibrium at that temperature.

children.label: i
children.question_text: Calculate the reaction quotient  \( Q_{c} \)  for the system under the initial conditions.
children.answer:
Reaction quotient  \( Q_{c} = [\mathrm{PCl}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})][\mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})] / [\mathrm{PCl}_{5}(\mathrm{~g})] \)
(State symbols not required) (Ignore incorrect state symbols)
\(  = (0.16 \div 4) \times (0.16 \div 4) \div (0.84 \div 4)  \)
\(  = 7.6 \times 10^{-3} \, \mathrm{mol} \, \mathrm{dm}^{-3}  \)  (Correct unit is required, not accept M)
(Accept:  \( 7.62 \times 10^{-3} \) ,  \( 7.619 \times 10^{-3} \) ,  \( 7.6190 \times 10^{-3} \) )
(1 mark for correct  \( Q_{c} \)  expression.
1 mark for substituting the correct data into the expression.
1 mark for correct final answer.)

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Explain whether the concentration of  \( PCl_{5}(g) \)  would increase or decrease just after the reaction started.
children.answer: Concentration of  \( PCl_{5} \)  would decrease. As  \( Q_{c} < K_{c} \) , the forward reaction rate is greater than the backward reaction rate.

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Explain whether  \( K_{c} \)  would increase, decrease or remain unchanged if the temperature of the equilibrium mixture is increased.
sub_questions.answer:
•  \( K_{c} \)  would increase.
• As the forward reaction is endothermic, an increase in temperature favours endothermic reaction / the equilibrium position shifts to the product side.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
At room conditions,  \( \mathrm{H_{2}O_{2}(aq)} \)  would decompose into  \( \mathrm{O_{2}(g)} \)  and  \( \mathrm{H_{2}O(l)} \)  very slowly in the absence of  \( \mathrm{MnO_{2}(s)} \) . An experiment was performed as shown in the set-up below:

[IMG:q10_img_0.png]

When 10.0 cm \( ^{3} \)  of 3.00 M H \( _{2} \) O \( _{2} \) (aq) was mixed with a small amount of MnO \( _{2} \) (s) and detergent solution at room conditions, O \( _{2} \) (g) started to be released rapidly and foam was produced. The MnO \( _{2} \) (s) remained chemically unchanged at the end of the reaction.

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Write a chemical equation for the decomposition of  \( H_{2}O_{2}(aq) \) .
sub_questions.answer:
\( 2H_{2}O_{2}(aq) \rightarrow 2H_{2}O(l) + O_{2}(g) \)
(State symbols not required)(Ignore incorrect state symbols)

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Explain how manganese illustrates a characteristic of transition metals according to the results of this experiment.
sub_questions.answer: Manganese/MnO\( _{2} \) illustrates catalytic property because the rate of formation of gas bubbles (O\( _{2}\)) increases / rate of the reaction increases when MnO\( _{2} \)(s) is present, and MnO\( _{2} \) remained chemically unchanged at the end of the reaction.

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text:
Upon completion of the reaction, all the  \( H_{2}O_{2}(aq) \)  was used up. Calculate the theoretical volume of  \( O_{2}(g) \)  released at room conditions.
(Molar volume of gas at room conditions = 24 dm\( ^{3} \) )
sub_questions.answer:
theoretical volume of  \( O_{2}(g) \)  released =  \( (3.00 \times 0.0100) \div 2 \times 24 \)
\(  = 0.36 \, \text{dm}^{3} \text{ or } 360 \, \text{cm}^{3}  \)
(Correct unit is required)

sub_questions.label: d
sub_questions.question_text: In the experiment, the time taken for the foam to rise from the mark at 100 cm \( ^{3} \)  to the mark at 200 cm \( ^{3} \)  of the measuring cylinder was 18 seconds, while the time taken for the foam to rise from the mark at 200 cm \( ^{3} \)  to the mark at 300 cm \( ^{3} \)  was 63 seconds. Explain these results.
sub_questions.answer:
• The rate decreases progressively during the reaction / The reaction becomes progressively slower as it takes longer time for foam to reach every  \( 100 \, cm^{3} \) -mark.
• Because the concentration of reactant ( \( H_{2}O_{2} \) ) decreases in the progress of the reaction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
Compounds P, Q and R are structural isomers having the molecular formula of  \( C_{5}H_{12}O \) . Their structures are shown below:

[IMG:q11_img_0.png]

sub_questions.label: a
sub_questions.question_text: Give the systematic name of P.
sub_questions.answer: \( \dagger \)  2-methylbutan-2-ol / 2-methyl-2-butanol

sub_questions.label: b
sub_questions.question_text: Heating Q with acidified  \( K_{2}Cr_{2}O_{7}(aq) \)  under reflux will give an organic product.
sub_questions.answer:
(i)

1 mark for correct diagram;
(The diagram should show the flask and the condenser are two pieces of glassware.)
(Not accept: closed system apparatus. E.g. condenser fitted with a stopper)
1 mark for correct labels: correct positions of water in, water out and heat
(Not accept: labelling heat with a triangle or an arrow only)

children.label: i
children.question_text: Draw a labelled diagram to show the set-up for this reaction.
children.answer:
[IMG:ans_q11_b_0.jpg]

1 mark for correct diagram.
(The diagram should show the flask and the condenser are two pieces of glassware.)
(Not accept: closed system apparatus. E.g. condenser fitted with a stopper)
1 mark for correct labels: correct positions of water in, water out and heat
(Not accept: labelling heat with a triangle or an arrow only)

children.label: ii
children.question_text: State the expected observation for this reaction.
children.answer: The reaction mixture changes from orange to green.

children.label: iii
children.question_text: Write the structural formula of the organic product.
children.answer: [IMG:ans_q11_ii_1.jpg]

sub_questions.label: c
sub_questions.question_text: W is an organic compound containing five carbon atoms. Under suitable conditions, R can be prepared from the reduction of W.

children.label: i
children.question_text: Suggest the structural formula of W.
children.answer: [IMG:ans_q11_c_2.jpg] or [IMG:ans_q11_c_3.jpg]

children.label: ii
children.question_text: Suggest a reducing agent required for the reaction.
children.answer:
LiAlH_{4} (if answered carboxylic acid in part (i))
Or, LiAlH_{4} / NaBH_{4} (if answered aldehyde in part (i))
(Not accept: LiAlH_{4} in acidic/aqueous medium, and NaBH_{4} in acidic medium.)

sub_questions.label: d
sub_questions.question_text: Compound S is an optically active secondary alcohol. It is also a structural isomer of compounds P, Q and R. Write the structural formula of S.
sub_questions.answer: [IMG:ans_q11_ii_4.jpg]

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question_text:
Outline a synthetic route, with NO MORE THAN THREE STEPS, to accomplish the following conversion. For each step, give the reagent(s), reaction conditions (as appropriate) and structure of the organic product.

[IMG:q12_img_0.png]
questions.answer:
[IMG:q12_img_1.png]

(intermediate: 1 mark; reagent: 1 mark for each step)
(For \(1^{st}\) step: Accept \(Al_2O_3\) with heat / conc. \(H_3PO_4\) with heat.)
(For \(2^{nd}\) step: If the intermediate is incorrect, but it still has a C=C double bond, and the scheme shows a correct hydrogenation reaction, 1 mark for the reagent if the reagent is correct.)
(Deduct 1 mark, if more than three steps are given.)

questions.subject: Chemistry
topics: Topic 12: Patterns in the Chemical World
questions.question_text:
Describe the acid-base properties of the products formed (if any) when the following oxides are added to water separately. Chemical equations are NOT required.

\( Na_{2}O \)   \( MgO \)   \( Al_{2}O_{3} \)   \( Cl_{2}O \)
questions.answer:
Chemical knowledge
•  \( Na_{2}O(s) \)  reacts vigorously with water / dissolve in water to form sodium hydroxide / NaOH(aq) which is (strongly) alkaline / basic.
• MgO(s) reacts slowly with water / is slightly soluble in water to form magnesium hydroxide / Mg(OH) \( _{2} \) (aq) which is (weakly) alkaline / basic.
•  \( Al_{2}O_{3}(s) \)  does not react with water / is insoluble / no product is formed.
• Cl₂O(g) reacts readily with water / dissolve in water to form hypochlorous acid / HOCl(aq) / HClO(aq) which is (weakly) acidic.
(Not accept: just mention  \( Na_{2}O(s) \)  &  \( MgO(s) \)  are basic oxides /  \( Al_{2}O_{3}(s) \)  is amphoteric oxide /  \( Cl_{2}O(g) \)  is acidic oxide.)
Communication mark
(Chemical knowledge = 0 to 2, communication mark = 0
Chemical knowledge = 3 to 4, communication mark = 0 or 1
Incomplete answer or difficult to understand, communication mark = 0
